title: America's been arguing over trade for more than 250 years
gist: PIIE trade historian Douglas Irwin charts 250 years of US trade openness and tariff levels, showing that intense fights over tariffs are the American baseline, not an aberration. He documents a long pendulum from 60% tariffs in 1828 to post-WWII free-trade lows, and frames the current Trump tariff escalation and post-2008 de-globalization as the latest swing in a recurring cycle.

insights.claim: US merchandise trade ran 10-20% of GDP in the decade after the 1787 Constitution, then fell to 5-10% after the War of 1812 as economic weight shifted to a less Europe-integrated Midwest and tariffs and domestic manufacturing reduced import dependence.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: The long-run trade/GDP baseline is structurally lower than headline globalization narratives imply; today's de-globalization is a return toward historical norms, not uncharted collapse.
insights.locator: Figure 1 presents one measure of the importance of merchandise trade

insights.claim: US trade as a share of GDP held near 5% from the Civil War through the end of the 19th century, then rose from about 5% to more than 10% starting in the 1970s during the globalization era.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Frames the 1970s-onward globalization boom as a distinct regime; a reversal back toward ~5% would be a multi-decade structural shift with broad asset implications.
insights.locator: After the war, trade settled down to between 5 and 10 percent of GDP

insights.claim: US imports exceeded exports for the first time in 1980, marking the onset of persistent large US trade deficits.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Dates the structural US deficit to 1980, useful context for gauging how entrenched the imbalance driving today's tariff politics actually is.
insights.quote: The globalization era also sees imports becoming greater than exports in 1980 as the United States begins to experience large trade deficits.
insights.locator: The globalization era also sees imports becoming greater than exports in 1980

insights.claim: US trade has been falling as a share of GDP since the 2008-10 global financial crisis, a de-globalization process accelerated by the tariffs of the two Trump administrations.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Signals a durable trade-intensity downtrend predating Trump; investors should treat reshoring/trade-share decline as a secular trend, not a single-administration policy spasm.
insights.locator: Since the global financial crisis of 2008-10, trade has been falling as a share of US GDP

insights.claim: US average tariffs rose from about 10% in 1790 to 60% under the Tariff of Abominations in 1828, provoking a crisis in which South Carolina and other Southern states threatened to nullify the tariff and nearly seceded.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Historical proof that extreme tariff levels are politically destabilizing and self-limiting; a benchmark for judging how far current tariffs can climb before triggering backlash.
insights.locator: Figure 2 shows the evolution of US tariffs over the course of history

insights.claim: The Compromise of 1833, brokered by Senator Henry Clay, brought US tariff levels down from their 1828 peak to under 20% by 1860.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Shows tariff spikes historically get negotiated back down within decades; a template for expecting eventual de-escalation after political crisis.
insights.locator: The Compromise of 1833, brokered by Senator Henry Clay

insights.claim: The Civil War shifted political power to the Northern Republican party, which instituted high tariffs both to raise revenue and to protect domestic industry.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Ties tariff regimes to which coalition holds power; the revenue-plus-protection rationale is the same dual justification invoked for tariffs today.
insights.locator: The Civil War shifted political power to the Republican party

insights.claim: After WWII and the creation of GATT, US tariffs stabilized and trended downward through free trade agreements such as NAFTA (in force 1994), before the Trump administration reversed course and tariff levels jumped significantly in 2025.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Frames 2025 as the end of an 80-year multilateral free-trade era; a regime break that reprices supply chains, trade-exposed equities, and FX.
insights.quote: However, the Trump administration has reversed many of these policies, and tariff levels jumped significantly in 2025.
insights.locator: After World War II and the establishment of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade

insights.claim: Many historical US duties were specific dollar amounts per imported quantity rather than ad valorem, so their effective percentage rate moved inversely with the price level, spiking in deflation and falling in inflation.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: A reminder that headline tariff rates are distorted by the inflation regime; useful when comparing modern ad valorem tariffs against historical series.
insights.locator: Many duties were specific (not ad valorem)

themes.theme: Trade as founding-era political fault line

insights.claim: British North American colonists depended on overseas trade for essential goods they could not produce locally, to the point that historians argue imports made colonial settlement viable, not merely comfortable.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Grounds the claim that trade dependence is foundational to the US economy, undercutting the notion that autarky is a natural or costless default.
insights.quote: Overseas commerce did not merely make colonial life comfortable, it made it possible.
insights.locator: From the early 1600s, British settlers in North America relied on overseas trade

insights.claim: Taxation of foreign trade was central to the American independence movement, with the 1776 Declaration of Independence citing Britain's cutting off colonial trade and imposing taxes without consent among its grievances.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Establishes that trade and tariff conflict is woven into US political origins, supporting the thesis that today's fights are the norm rather than a crisis to be resolved.

themes.theme: Implied read on today's tariff politics

insights.claim: A PIIE trade historian argues that intense American disagreement over tariffs is a 250-year-old constant, implying the current fights over Trump-era tariffs will persist for years rather than resolve into a stable consensus.
insights.speaker: Douglas A. Irwin
insights.so_what: Sets an expectation of sustained trade-policy volatility as a base case; investors should price ongoing tariff uncertainty rather than betting on quick normalization.
insights.quote: This suggests that the nation's trade debates are sure to continue for years to come.
insights.locator: Today, Americans disagree intensely about the Trump administration's use of tariffs
